summarization.a: Shows a 3D schematic of a close-proximity spatial ALD (SALD) gas injection head positioned above a substrate, illustrating injector geometry and proximity to the surface.
summarization.b: Presents COMSOL-simulated gas velocity distribution beneath the injector, highlighting flow confinement, exhaust channels, and alternating N₂, DEZ, and H₂O injection zones.
summarization.c: Displays the precursor concentration distribution near the substrate, demonstrating spatial separation of reactants and effective exhaust removal to prevent gas-phase mixing.

data_extraction.b:
| Gas | Velocity |
| --- | --- |
| N₂ | High |
| DEZ | Medium |
| H₂O | Low |
| Exhaust | Low |
data_extraction.c:
| Gas | Concentration |
| --- | --- |
| N₂ | Low |
| DEZ | High |
| H₂O | Low |
| Exhaust | Low |

a.question: Does the close-proximity SALD injector enable spatially separated delivery of precursors to the substrate surface?

c.question: Which structural features of the injector govern gas flow and precursor confinement?
c.answer: Alternating precursor and N₂ injection channels, Adjacent exhaust channels, Gap distance between injector head and substrate, Channel spacing (d_DEZ, d_H₂O)

b.question: How does gas behavior differ between the velocity and precursor concentration maps beneath the injector?
b.answer: Gas velocity peaks near injection and exhaust channels, while precursor concentration is localized above the substrate and confined between adjacent exhausts.

b.question: Why is controlling gas velocity and precursor concentration critical for spatial ALD performance?
b.answer: Precise control of gas velocity and precursor concentration ensures effective spatial separation of reactants, preventing parasitic gas-phase reactions. This enables continuous, high-throughput ALD while maintaining self-limiting surface reactions, uniform film growth, and high deposition selectivity across the substrate.
